Catalog Description

Senior Capstone Design. (1-6). Credit 3. Development of system integration skills for

solving real-world problems in computer science; significant team software project

that uses integration of advanced concepts across computer science

specializations; projects require design, implementation, documentation and

demonstration, as well as design methodology, management process and

teamwork. Prerequisite: Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or CSCE

331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

Course Prerequisites

Prerequisite/Corequisite(s):  Grade of C or better in CSCE 411, and CSCE 315 or

CSCE 331; senior classification; also taught at Galveston campus.

Course Learning Outcomes

Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

Analyze a complex computing problem and apply principles of computing

and other relevant disciplines to identify solutions.

Design, implement and evaluate a computing-based solution to meet a given

set of computing requirements in the context of the program's discipline.

Communicate effectively in a variety of professional contexts.

Recognize professional responsibilities and make informed judgements in

computing practice based on legal and ethical principles.

Collaborate effectively as a member or leader of a team engaged in activities

appropriate to the program's discipline.

Apply computer science theory and software implementation fundamentals

to produce computing-based solutions.

Special Course Designation

UCRT - University Required Oral Communication

To receive C credit for this course, you must pass the C component, with the C course

communication requirement below:

appropriate to the program's discipline.

Apply computer science theory and software implementation fundamentals

to produce computing-based solutions.

Special Course Designation

UCRT - University Required Oral Communication

To receive C credit for this course, you must pass the C component, with the C course

communication requirement below:

A minimum of  35% of your final grade  is based on written and oral communication

assignments.

Of this communication component,  at least 70% is based on individually written

work and individual public speaking ; group work does not count toward this

standard.

To receive C credit, students must pass both the written and public speaking

components of the course.  Failure to pass either component will result in failing the

course, regardless of performance on other assignments.

Each student will produce at least 1250 words of individually written work and

deliver a minimum of 5 minutes of individual public speaking.

Assignments fulfilling this requirement include individual progress reports, project

proposals, final technical documentation, and individual presentations or

demonstrations.

Resumes and related materials are accepted only if specific to the major; take-home

essay exams may count if they allow drafting and revision. Oral presentation slides,

scripts, web sites, and posters count when accompanied by individual presentations.

No more than  10% of the communication grade may be assessed by undergraduate aides ,

and all grading by aides is supervised by the instructor.

Grading Policy

The final grade you will receive in the class will be based on points accumulated

during the semester. The grading scale is as follows: F < 60%  ≤  D < 70%  ≤  C < 80%  ≤

B < 90%  ≤  A

Course project 1: 25%. Due 6th week.

Course project 2: 30%. Due 10th week.

Course project 3: 35%. Due 14th week.

Attendance: 10%.

Late Work Policy

Work submitted by a student as makeup work for an excused absence is not

considered late work and is exempt from the late work policy ( Student Rule 7 ).

Late work is accepted but deducted 0.5% per late hour.

Additional Course Information

Course Expectations

Course expectations, in the form of a course appreciative agreement, will be reviewed and

agreed to in the first 2 weeks of class.

The course provides  direct instruction in writing and public speaking  through lectures,

workshops, peer review, assignment models, and opportunities for practice and revision.

Formative feedback  will be given on both writing and speaking assignments prior to final

grading, and students are expected to revise and improve major submissions and

presentations in response to instructor and peer evaluations.

AI Statement

Writing: Use of AI tools, including ChatGPT, is permitted in this course for students who wish to

use them. To adhere to our scholarly values, students must cite any AI-generated material that

informed their work (this includes in-text citations and/or use of quotations, and in your

reference list). Using an AI tool to generate content without proper attribution qualifies as

academic dishonesty.

See  https://www.chicagomanualofstyle.org/qanda/data/faq/topics/Documentation/faq0422.

html  for information how to properly cite AI tools. (adapted from University of Massachusetts

Amherst,  https://cte.tamu.edu/getmedia/1d5e4ef6-97f1-4065-987f-3c9dfecbb7bd/TAMU-

CTE_GenAI-SyllabusStatementConsiderations.pdf )

Computer Code. In principle you may submit AI-generated code, or code that is based on or

derived from AI-generated code, as long as this use is properly documented in the comments:

you need to include the prompt and the significant parts of the response. AI tools may help

you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is correct. It is your

responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing.

Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for

improvement, in particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The

you avoid syntax errors, but there is no guarantee that the generated code is correct. It is your

responsibility to identify errors in program logic through comprehensive, documented testing.

Moreover, generated code, even if syntactically correct, may have significant scope for

improvement, in particular regarding separation of concerns and avoiding repetitions. The

submission itself must meet our standards of attribution and validation.(from Boris Steipe

(2023) “Syllabus Resources”. The Sentient Syllabus Project  http://sentientsyllabus.org  .)

Course Specific Attendance Policy

Attendance is important.  Not attending weekly class meetings harms the other members of

your group and makes it much more difficult for the instructor to assess your contributions to

the group effort. Due to the undue burden your frequent absences have on the rest of the

team,  more than five unexcused absences are an automatic failure .

The instructors will evaluate your attendance through frequent quizzes, meetings,

participation in the discussions, participation in assignments, and/or contributions to the

team. Upon instructors’ discretion, arriving to class late (> 5 minutes late), leaving early (> 5

minutes before class end), and/or not participating (e.g., not taking a quiz, not being present

for a status meeting, not submitting in class work, and/or doing work not related to the class)

can be considered an unexcused absence, depending on the circumstances. Oversleeping, a

term paper due, an exam to cram for, etc., are not valid reasons. The instructor reserves the

right to determine what constitutes a “valid reason” on a case-by-case basis.

Absence / lateness during days in which teams may need to work in a remote location to

accomplish certain assignments (e.g., presenting your work outside of class, interviewing

experts, meeting with mentors, accessing technologies outside of the classroom, etc.) count

toward the 5 unexcused absences, unless the absence / lateness are approved by the

instructor in advance. Students are expected to meet instructor’s requirements for check in,

activity reporting requirements, and/or timely submission of-class assignments.

You are responsible for monitoring / tracking your unexcused absences.

Students experiencing personal injury or illness that is too severe for the student to attend

class qualify for an excused absence (see  Student Rule 7, Section 7.2.2 ) assuming that

documentation and notification guidelines are followed.
